question:
Evaluate
\[
\lim_{x \to 1^-} \prod_{n=0}^\infty \left(\frac{1 + x^{n+1}}{1 +
x^n}\right)^{x^n}.
\]
solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
By taking logarithms, we see that the desired limit is $\exp(L)$,
where $L = \lim_{x\to 1^-} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty} x^n \left(
\ln(1+x^{n+1}) - \ln(1+x^n) \right)$. Now
\begin{align*}
&\sum_{n=0}^N x^n \left( \ln(1+x^{n+1}) - \ln(1+x^n) \right) \\
& = 1/x
\sum_{n=0}^N x^{n+1} \ln(1+x^{n+1}) - \sum_{n=0}^N x^n\ln(1+x^n) \\
&=
x^N \ln(1+x^{N+1}) - \ln 2 + (1/x-1) \sum_{n=1}^N x^n\ln(1+x^n);
\end{align*}
since $\lim_{N\to\infty} (x^N\ln(1+x^{N+1})) = 0$ for $0<x<1$, we
conclude that $L = - \ln 2 + \lim_{x\to 1^-} f(x)$, where
\begin{align*}
f(x) &= (1/x-1) \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} x^n\ln(1+x^n) \\
&= (1/x-1)
\sum_{n=1}^\infty \sum_{m=1}^\infty (-1)^{m+1} x^{n+mn}/m.
\end{align*}
This final double sum converges absolutely when $0<x<1$, since
\begin{align*}
\sum_{n=1}^\infty \sum_{m=1}^\infty x^{n+mn}/m &= \sum_{n=1}^\infty
x^n (-\ln(1-x^n)) \\
&< \sum_{n=1}^\infty x^n (-\ln(1-x)),
\end{align*}
which converges. (Note that $-\ln(1-x)$ and $-\ln(1-x^n)$ are
positive.) Hence we may interchange the summations in $f(x)$ to
obtain
\begin{align*}
f(x) &= (1/x-1) \sum_{m=1}^\infty \sum_{n=1}^\infty \frac{(-1)^{m+1}
x^{(m+1)n}}{m} \\
&= (1/x-1) \sum_{m=1}^\infty \frac{(-1)^{m+1}}
{m}\left(\frac{x^m(1-x)}{1-x^{m+1}}\right).
\end{align*}
This last sum converges absolutely uniformly in $x$, so it is legitimate
to take limits term by term.
Since $\lim_{x\to 1^-} \frac{x^m{1-x}}{1-x^{m+1}} = \frac{1}{m+1}$
for fixed $m$, we have
\begin{align*}
\lim_{x\to 1^-} f(x) &= \sum_{m=1}^\infty \frac{(-1)^{m+1}}{m(m+1)} \\
&= \sum_{m=1}^\infty (-1)^{m+1}\left( \frac{1}{m}-\frac{1}{m+1} \right) \\
&= 2 \left( \sum_{m=1}^\infty \frac{(-1)^{m+1}}{m} \right) - 1 \\ &= 2
\ln 2 - 1,
\end{align*}
and hence $L = \ln 2 - 1$ and the desired limit is $2/e$.

\textbf{Remark:} Note that the last series is not absolutely convergent,
so the recombination must be done without rearranging terms.

\textbf{Second solution:}
(by Greg Price, via Tony Zhang and Anders Kaseorg)
Put $t_n(x) = \ln(1 + x^n)$;
we can then write $x^n = \exp(t_n(x)) - 1$, and
\[
L = \lim_{x \to 1^-} \sum_{n=0}^\infty (t_n(x) - t_{n+1}(x))(1 - \exp(t_n(x))).
\]
The expression on the right is a Riemann sum approximating the
integral $\int_0^{\ln 2} (1-e^t)\,dt$, over the subdivision of $[0,\ln(2))$
given by the $t_n(x)$. As $x \to 1^-$, the maximum difference between
consecutive $t_n(x)$ tends to 0, so the Riemann sum tends to the value
of the integral. Hence $L = \int_0^{\ln 2}(1 - e^t)\,dt = \ln 2 - 1$, as
desired.

\def\calA{\mathcal{A}}
\def\calB{\mathcal{B}}

descriptive_long.question:
Evaluate
\[
\lim_{variable \to 1^-} \prod_{indexone=0}^\infty \left(\frac{1 + variable^{indexone+1}}{1 +
variable^{indexone}}\right)^{variable^{indexone}}.
\]
descriptive_long.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
By taking logarithms, we see that the desired limit is $\exp(limitval)$,
where $limitval = \lim_{variable\to 1^-} \sum_{indexone=0}^{\infty} variable^{indexone} \left(
\ln(1+variable^{indexone+1}) - \ln(1+variable^{indexone}) \right)$. Now
\begin{align*}
&\sum_{indexone=0}^{indexbig} variable^{indexone} \left( \ln(1+variable^{indexone+1}) - \ln(1+variable^{indexone}) \right) \\
& = 1/variable
\sum_{indexone=0}^{indexbig} variable^{indexone+1} \ln(1+variable^{indexone+1}) - \sum_{indexone=0}^{indexbig} variable^{indexone}\ln(1+variable^{indexone}) \\
&=
variable^{indexbig} \ln(1+variable^{indexbig+1}) - \ln 2 + (1/variable-1) \sum_{indexone=1}^{indexbig} variable^{indexone}\ln(1+variable^{indexone});
\end{align*}
since $\lim_{indexbig\to\infty} (variable^{indexbig}\ln(1+variable^{indexbig+1})) = 0$ for $0<variable<1$, we
conclude that $limitval = - \ln 2 + \lim_{variable\to 1^-} helperf(variable)$, where
\begin{align*}
helperf(variable) &= (1/variable-1) \sum_{indexone=1}^{\infty} variable^{indexone}\ln(1+variable^{indexone}) \\
&= (1/variable-1)
\sum_{indexone=1}^\infty \sum_{indextwo=1}^\infty (-1)^{indextwo+1} variable^{indexone+indextwo indexone}/indextwo.
\end{align*}
This final double sum converges absolutely when $0<variable<1$, since
\begin{align*}
\sum_{indexone=1}^\infty \sum_{indextwo=1}^\infty variable^{indexone+indextwo indexone}/indextwo &= \sum_{indexone=1}^\infty
variable^{indexone} (-\ln(1-variable^{indexone})) \\
&< \sum_{indexone=1}^\infty variable^{indexone} (-\ln(1-variable)),
\end{align*}
which converges. (Note that $-\ln(1-variable)$ and $-\ln(1-variable^{indexone})$ are
positive.) Hence we may interchange the summations in $helperf(variable)$ to
obtain
\begin{align*}
helperf(variable) &= (1/variable-1) \sum_{indextwo=1}^\infty \sum_{indexone=1}^\infty \frac{(-1)^{indextwo+1}
variable^{(indextwo+1)indexone}}{indextwo} \\
&= (1/variable-1) \sum_{indextwo=1}^\infty \frac{(-1)^{indextwo+1}}
{indextwo}\left(\frac{variable^{indextwo}(1-variable)}{1-variable^{indextwo+1}}\right).
\end{align*}
This last sum converges absolutely uniformly in variable, so it is legitimate
to take limits term by term.
Since $\lim_{variable\to 1^-} \frac{variable^{indextwo}{1-variable}}{1-variable^{indextwo+1}} = \frac{1}{indextwo+1}$
for fixed indextwo, we have
\begin{align*}
\lim_{variable\to 1^-} helperf(variable) &= \sum_{indextwo=1}^\infty \frac{(-1)^{indextwo+1}}{indextwo(indextwo+1)} \\
&= \sum_{indextwo=1}^\infty (-1)^{indextwo+1}\left( \frac{1}{indextwo}-\frac{1}{indextwo+1} \right) \\
&= 2 \left( \sum_{indextwo=1}^\infty \frac{(-1)^{indextwo+1}}{indextwo} \right) - 1 \\ &= 2
\ln 2 - 1,
\end{align*}
and hence $limitval = \ln 2 - 1$ and the desired limit is $2/e$.

\textbf{Remark:} Note that the last series is not absolutely convergent,
so the recombination must be done without rearranging terms.

\textbf{Second solution:}
(by Greg Price, via Tony Zhang and Anders Kaseorg)
Put $tempfunc(variable) = \ln(1 + variable^{indexone})$;
we can then write $variable^{indexone} = \exp(tempfunc(variable)) - 1$, and
\[
limitval = \lim_{variable \to 1^-} \sum_{indexone=0}^\infty (tempfunc(variable) - tempfuncnext(variable))(1 - \exp(tempfunc(variable))).
\]
The expression on the right is a Riemann sum approximating the
integral $\int_0^{\ln 2} (1-e^{tempvar})\,dtempvar$, over the subdivision of $[0,\ln(2))$
given by the $tempfunc(variable)$. As $variable \to 1^-$, the maximum difference between
consecutive $tempfunc(variable)$ tends to 0, so the Riemann sum tends to the value
of the integral. Hence $limitval = \int_0^{\ln 2}(1 - e^{tempvar})\,dtempvar = \ln 2 - 1$, as
desired.

\def\calA{\mathcal{A}}
\def\calB{\mathcal{B}}

descriptive_long_confusing.question:
Evaluate
\[
\lim_{carousel \to 1^-} \prod_{labyrinth=0}^\infty \left(\frac{1 + carousel^{labyrinth+1}}{1 +
carousel^{labyrinth}}\right)^{carousel^{labyrinth}}.
\]

descriptive_long_misleading.question:
Evaluate
\[
\lim_{steadyvalue \to 1^-} \prod_{wholeindex=0}^\infty \left(\frac{1 + steadyvalue^{wholeindex+1}}{1 +
steadyvalue^{wholeindex}}\right)^{steadyvalue^{wholeindex}}.
\]
descriptive_long_misleading.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
By taking logarithms, we see that the desired limit is $\exp(beginvalue)$,
where $beginvalue = \lim_{steadyvalue\to 1^-} \sum_{wholeindex=0}^{\infty} steadyvalue^{wholeindex} \left(
\ln(1+steadyvalue^{wholeindex+1}) - \ln(1+steadyvalue^{wholeindex}) \right)$. Now
\begin{align*}
&\sum_{wholeindex=0}^{lowerbound} steadyvalue^{wholeindex} \left( \ln(1+steadyvalue^{wholeindex+1}) - \ln(1+steadyvalue^{wholeindex}) \right) \\
& = 1/steadyvalue
\sum_{wholeindex=0}^{lowerbound} steadyvalue^{wholeindex+1} \ln(1+steadyvalue^{wholeindex+1}) - \sum_{wholeindex=0}^{lowerbound} steadyvalue^{wholeindex}\ln(1+steadyvalue^{wholeindex}) \\
&=
steadyvalue^{lowerbound} \ln(1+steadyvalue^{lowerbound+1}) - \ln 2 + (1/steadyvalue-1) \sum_{wholeindex=1}^{lowerbound} steadyvalue^{wholeindex}\ln(1+steadyvalue^{wholeindex});
\end{align*}
since $\lim_{lowerbound\to\infty} (steadyvalue^{lowerbound}\ln(1+steadyvalue^{lowerbound+1})) = 0$ for $0<steadyvalue<1$, we
conclude that $beginvalue = - \ln 2 + \lim_{steadyvalue\to 1^-} constant(steadyvalue)$, where
\begin{align*}
constant(steadyvalue) &= (1/steadyvalue-1) \sum_{wholeindex=1}^{\infty} steadyvalue^{wholeindex}\ln(1+steadyvalue^{wholeindex}) \\
&= (1/steadyvalue-1)
\sum_{wholeindex=1}^\infty \sum_{partindex=1}^\infty (-1)^{partindex+1} steadyvalue^{wholeindex+partindexwholeindex}/partindex.
\end{align*}
This final double sum converges absolutely when $0<steadyvalue<1$, since
\begin{align*}
\sum_{wholeindex=1}^\infty \sum_{partindex=1}^\infty steadyvalue^{wholeindex+partindexwholeindex}/partindex &= \sum_{wholeindex=1}^\infty
steadyvalue^{wholeindex} (-\ln(1-steadyvalue^{wholeindex})) \\
&< \sum_{wholeindex=1}^\infty steadyvalue^{wholeindex} (-\ln(1-steadyvalue)),
\end{align*}
which converges. (Note that $-\ln(1-steadyvalue)$ and $-\ln(1-steadyvalue^{wholeindex})$ are
positive.) Hence we may interchange the summations in $constant(steadyvalue)$ to
obtain
\begin{align*}
constant(steadyvalue) &= (1/steadyvalue-1) \sum_{partindex=1}^\infty \sum_{wholeindex=1}^\infty \frac{(-1)^{partindex+1}
steadyvalue^{(partindex+1)wholeindex}}{partindex} \\
&= (1/steadyvalue-1) \sum_{partindex=1}^\infty \frac{(-1)^{partindex+1}}
{partindex}\left(\frac{steadyvalue^{partindex}(1-steadyvalue)}{1-steadyvalue^{partindex+1}}\right).
\end{align*}
This last sum converges absolutely uniformly in $steadyvalue$, so it is legitimate
to take limits term by term.
Since $\lim_{steadyvalue\to 1^-} \frac{steadyvalue^{partindex}{1-steadyvalue}}{1-steadyvalue^{partindex+1}} = \frac{1}{partindex+1}$
for fixed $partindex$, we have
\begin{align*}
\lim_{steadyvalue\to 1^-} constant(steadyvalue) &= \sum_{partindex=1}^\infty \frac{(-1)^{partindex+1}}{partindex(partindex+1)} \\
&= \sum_{partindex=1}^\infty (-1)^{partindex+1}\left( \frac{1}{partindex}-\frac{1}{partindex+1} \right) \\
&= 2 \left( \sum_{partindex=1}^\infty \frac{(-1)^{partindex+1}}{partindex} \right) - 1 \\ &= 2
\ln 2 - 1,
\end{align*}
and hence $beginvalue = \ln 2 - 1$ and the desired limit is $2/e$.

\textbf{Remark:} Note that the last series is not absolutely convergent,
so the recombination must be done without rearranging terms.

\textbf{Second solution:}
(by Greg Price, via Tony Zhang and Anders Kaseorg)
Put $staticterm(steadyvalue) = \ln(1 + steadyvalue^{wholeindex})$;
we can then write $steadyvalue^{wholeindex} = \exp(staticterm(steadyvalue)) - 1$, and
\[
beginvalue = \lim_{steadyvalue \to 1^-} \sum_{wholeindex=0}^\infty (staticterm(steadyvalue) - statictermnext(steadyvalue))(1 - \exp(staticterm(steadyvalue))).
\]
The expression on the right is a Riemann sum approximating the
integral $\int_0^{\ln 2} (1-e^{spacevar})\,dspacevar$, over the subdivision of $[0,\ln(2))$
given by the $staticterm(steadyvalue)$. As $steadyvalue \to 1^-$, the maximum difference between
consecutive $staticterm(steadyvalue)$ tends to 0, so the Riemann sum tends to the value
of the integral. Hence $beginvalue = \int_0^{\ln 2}(1 - e^{spacevar})\,dspacevar = \ln 2 - 1$, as
desired.

\def\calA{\mathcal{A}}
\def\calB{\mathcal{B}}

garbled_string.question:
Evaluate
\[
\lim_{kdfjowpq \to 1^-} \prod_{wlerizua=0}^\infty \left(\frac{1 + kdfjowpq^{wlerizua+1}}{1 +
kdfjowpq^{wlerizua}}\right)^{kdfjowpq^{wlerizua}}.
\]
garbled_string.solution:
\textbf{First solution:}
By taking logarithms, we see that the desired limit is $\exp(\zdnuvyso)$,
where $\zdnuvyso = \lim_{kdfjowpq\to 1^-} \sum_{wlerizua=0}^{\infty} kdfjowpq^{wlerizua} \left(
\ln(1+kdfjowpq^{wlerizua+1}) - \ln(1+kdfjowpq^{wlerizua}) \right)$. Now
\begin{align*}
&\sum_{wlerizua=0}^{\ruvyqasp} kdfjowpq^{wlerizua} \left( \ln(1+kdfjowpq^{wlerizua+1}) - \ln(1+kdfjowpq^{wlerizua}) \right) \\
& = 1/kdfjowpq
\sum_{wlerizua=0}^{\ruvyqasp} kdfjowpq^{wlerizua+1} \ln(1+kdfjowpq^{wlerizua+1}) - \sum_{wlerizua=0}^{\ruvyqasp} kdfjowpq^{wlerizua}\ln(1+kdfjowpq^{wlerizua}) \\
&=
kdfjowpq^{\ruvyqasp} \ln(1+kdfjowpq^{\ruvyqasp+1}) - \ln 2 + (1/kdfjowpq-1) \sum_{wlerizua=1}^{\ruvyqasp} kdfjowpq^{wlerizua}\ln(1+kdfjowpq^{wlerizua});
\end{align*}
since $\lim_{\ruvyqasp\to\infty} (kdfjowpq^{\ruvyqasp}\ln(1+kdfjowpq^{\ruvyqasp+1})) = 0$ for $0<kdfjowpq<1$, we
conclude that $\zdnuvyso = - \ln 2 + \lim_{kdfjowpq\to 1^-} \basumtek(kdfjowpq)$, where
\begin{align*}
\basumtek(kdfjowpq) &= (1/kdfjowpq-1) \sum_{wlerizua=1}^{\infty} kdfjowpq^{wlerizua}\ln(1+kdfjowpq^{wlerizua}) \\
&= (1/kdfjowpq-1)
\sum_{wlerizua=1}^\infty \sum_{ghtabnxc=1}^\infty (-1)^{ghtabnxc+1} kdfjowpq^{wlerizua+ghtabnxc wlerizua}/ghtabnxc.
\end{align*}
This final double sum converges absolutely when $0<kdfjowpq<1$, since
\begin{align*}
\sum_{wlerizua=1}^\infty \sum_{ghtabnxc=1}^\infty kdfjowpq^{wlerizua+ghtabnxc wlerizua}/ghtabnxc &= \sum_{wlerizua=1}^\infty
kdfjowpq^{wlerizua} (-\ln(1-kdfjowpq^{wlerizua})) \\
&< \sum_{wlerizua=1}^\infty kdfjowpq^{wlerizua} (-\ln(1-kdfjowpq)),
\end{align*}
which converges. (Note that $-\ln(1-kdfjowpq)$ and $-\ln(1-kdfjowpq^{wlerizua})$ are
positive.) Hence we may interchange the summations in $\basumtek(kdfjowpq)$ to
obtain
\begin{align*}
\basumtek(kdfjowpq) &= (1/kdfjowpq-1) \sum_{ghtabnxc=1}^\infty \sum_{wlerizua=1}^\infty \frac{(-1)^{ghtabnxc+1}
kdfjowpq^{(ghtabnxc+1)wlerizua}}{ghtabnxc} \\
&= (1/kdfjowpq-1) \sum_{ghtabnxc=1}^\infty \frac{(-1)^{ghtabnxc+1}}
{ghtabnxc}\left(\frac{kdfjowpq^{ghtabnxc}(1-kdfjowpq)}{1-kdfjowpq^{ghtabnxc+1}}\right).
\end{align*}
This last sum converges absolutely uniformly in $kdfjowpq$, so it is legitimate
to take limits term by term.
Since $\lim_{kdfjowpq\to 1^-} \frac{kdfjowpq^{ghtabnxc}{1-kdfjowpq}}{1-kdfjowpq^{ghtabnxc+1}} = \frac{1}{ghtabnxc+1}$
for fixed $ghtabnxc$, we have
\begin{align*}
\lim_{kdfjowpq\to 1^-} \basumtek(kdfjowpq) &= \sum_{ghtabnxc=1}^\infty \frac{(-1)^{ghtabnxc+1}}{ghtabnxc(ghtabnxc+1)} \\
&= \sum_{ghtabnxc=1}^\infty (-1)^{ghtabnxc+1}\left( \frac{1}{ghtabnxc}-\frac{1}{ghtabnxc+1} \right) \\
&= 2 \left( \sum_{ghtabnxc=1}^\infty \frac{(-1)^{ghtabnxc+1}}{ghtabnxc} \right) - 1 \\ &= 2
\ln 2 - 1,
\end{align*}
and hence $\zdnuvyso = \ln 2 - 1$ and the desired limit is $2/e$.

\textbf{Remark:} Note that the last series is not absolutely convergent,
so the recombination must be done without rearranging terms.

\textbf{Second solution:}
(by Greg Price, via Tony Zhang and Anders Kaseorg)
Put $\hjgbafmz(kdfjowpq) = \ln(1 + kdfjowpq^{wlerizua})$;
we can then write $kdfjowpq^{wlerizua} = \exp(\hjgbafmz(kdfjowpq)) - 1$, and
\[
\zdnuvyso = \lim_{kdfjowpq \to 1^-} \sum_{wlerizua=0}^\infty (\hjgbafmz(kdfjowpq) - \qlerdxtz(kdfjowpq))(1 - \exp(\hjgbafmz(kdfjowpq))).
\]
The expression on the right is a Riemann sum approximating the
integral $\int_0^{\ln 2} (1-e^{\poicmezl})\,d\poicmezl$, over the subdivision of $[0,\ln(2))$
given by the $\hjgbafmz(kdfjowpq)$. As $kdfjowpq \to 1^-$, the maximum difference between
consecutive $\hjgbafmz(kdfjowpq)$ tends to 0, so the Riemann sum tends to the value
of the integral. Hence $\zdnuvyso = \int_0^{\ln 2}(1 - e^{\poicmezl})\,d\poicmezl = \ln 2 - 1$, as
desired.

\def\calB{\mathcal{B}}

kernel_variant.question:
Evaluate
\[
\lim_{x\to 1^-} \prod_{n=2}^{\infty}\left(\frac{2 + x^{n+2}}{2 + x^{n}}\right)^{x^{n}}.
\]
kernel_variant.solution:
Define
  P(x)=\prod _{n=2}^\infty \Bigl(\frac{2+x^{n+2}}{2+x^n}\Bigr)^{x^n},
so we seek L=lim_{x\to 1^-}P(x).  Take logarithms:
  L(x)=ln P(x)=\sum _{n=2}^\infty  x^n[ln(2+x^{n+2})-ln(2+x^n)].
Telescoping gives
  L(x)=-x^2ln(2+x^2)-x^3ln(2+x^3)
        +\bigl(1/x^2-1\bigr)\sum_{n=4}^\infty  x^n ln(2+x^n).
Next expand
  ln(2+x^n)=ln 2+\sum _{m=1}^\infty (-1)^{m+1}x^{mn}/(m\cdot 2^m),
so that
  \sum _{n=4}^\infty  x^n ln(2+x^n)
    =ln2\cdot x^4/(1-x)
     +\sum _{m=1}^\infty (-1)^{m+1}/(m\cdot 2^m)\cdot x^{4(m+1)}/(1-x^{m+1}).
As x\to 1^- one checks:
  x^2ln(2+x^2)+x^3ln(2+x^3)\to 2 ln 3,
  (1/x^2-1)~2(1-x),
  x^4/(1-x)~1/(1-x),
  x^{4(m+1)}/(1-x^{m+1})~1/[(m+1)(1-x)].
Hence the ln 2-term contributes 2 ln 2, and the m-sum contributes
  2\sum_{m=1}^\infty (-1)^{m+1}/[m(m+1)2^m].
Set
  S=\sum _{m=1}^\infty (-1)^{m+1}/[m(m+1)2^m].
One finds
  \sum _{m=1}^\infty (-1)^{m+1}/(m 2^m)=ln(3/2),
  \sum _{m=1}^\infty (-1)^{m+1}/((m+1)2^m)=-2 ln(3/2)+1,
so S=3 ln(3/2)-1.  Therefore
  L=lim_{x\to 1^-}L(x)=-2 ln 3+2 ln 2+2S
    =4 ln(3/2)-2 = ln((3/2)^4)-2.
Exponentiating gives the desired limit
  lim_{x\to 1^-}P(x)=e^L=(3/2)^4 e^{-2}=(81/16)e^{-2}.

core_steps: Logarithm converts the infinite product into a single series.
A summation-by-parts (telescoping) rewrite isolates a factor (1/xŌłÆ1) times Ōłæ xŌü┐ ln(1+xŌü┐).
Absolute convergence lets the two infinite sums be interchanged safely.
Take the term-by-term limit as xŌåÆ1Ōü╗ to obtain an alternating series with rational terms.
Evaluate that alternating telescoping series (giving ln 2ŌłÆ1) and exponentiate to get the final limit.

slot1.description: The constant added inside the logarithm, i.e. the ŌĆ£+1ŌĆØ in ln(1+xŌü┐). Replacing it by any fixed positive constant c would carry the same argument through (yielding ln(c+1) in place of ln 2).

slot2.description: The one-step index shift between numerator and denominator, i.e. using n+1 versus n. Any fixed shift kŌēź1 (so numerator uses n+k) still permits the same telescoping manipulation.

slot3.description: The starting index of the product/series. Beginning at n=m instead of 0 only changes the constant term but leaves the chain of lemmas untouched.
